title: Implement SSO-triggered OAuth retry
description:
When GitHub returns 403 with x-github-sso header, automatically trigger OAuth Device Flow and retry request.

Implementation steps:
1. Create SSOAuthRequiredException
2. Update rest_service.py to raise typed exception
3. Add refresh token support to GitHubOAuth
4. Override get_request in GithubREST to catch SSO and retry
5. Add comprehensive tests

This allows PAT to coexist with OAuth token - if PAT fails SSO, OAuth is tried automatically.

title: Port FlexiDB core classes
description:
Port the FlexiDB in-memory indexed database and supporting classes. This is a prerequisite for Phase 5 (SprintReportTeamAnalysis). Includes:
- FlexiDB.groovy (~292 lines) - main database with indexing
- FlexiDBRow - dict-like row with defaults
- FlexiDBQueryColumn, FlexiDBIndexKey - query/index support
- Exception classes
- Init classes for column definitions

title: Coerce null GitHub commit additions/deletions to 0 (avoid TypeError aborting whole PR)
description: GitHub GraphQL returns additions/deletions=null (SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE) for large commits. github_rest.py get_pull_request_full normalizes with c.get('additions', 0) which returns None for explicit null (default only applies to missing keys). Downstream 'None > 0' in the commit loop raises TypeError, aborting the entire PR (marked failed, never retried). Fix: c.get('additions') or 0.
close_reason: Coerce null/missing GitHub commit additions/deletions to 0 at normalization in github_rest.py; prevents None>0 TypeError that aborted whole PR

title: Restore large initial GraphQL commit page size; small ladder only for failed-ticket retries
description: Commit 75ca152 shrank _COMMIT_PAGE_SIZES [20,10,5,2]->[2,1], dropping initial PR commit fetches from page size 20 to 2. Split into _COMMIT_PAGE_SIZES_INITIAL=[20,10,5,2,1] (fresh pulls) and _COMMIT_PAGE_SIZES_RETRY=[2,1] (failed-ticket retry pass). Add set_retry_mode to GraphQL client, delegate via GithubREST, toggle in the retry_issues branch.
close_reason: Split commit page-size ladder: large [20,10,5,2,1] for fresh pulls, small [2,1] only for failed-ticket retry pass via set_retry_mode

title: Track per-commit data (COMMIT_DATA) in sprint cache and sum at report time
description: Replace DBData.COMMIT_MESSAGES (list of message strings) with COMMIT_DATA (list of {message, additions, deletions} per commit). Report generation sums COMMIT_DATA to produce displayed COMMIT_ADDED/COMMIT_REMOVED, enabling per-commit --maxCommitSize filtering at report time instead of all-or-nothing per-PR suppression. Bump CACHE_VERSION 1.5->2.0.
close_reason: Implemented COMMIT_DATA per-commit tracking; report sums per-commit with per-commit maxCommitSize filtering; cache v2.0

title: Auto-enter Kanban mode when --kanbanCycleLength provided
description: When a Kanban board is detected (400 error) and --kanbanCycleLength is provided via command line, automatically enter Kanban mode instead of raising an error. Allows non-interactive Kanban processing: python -m ... -t TeamName -l 4 --kanbanCycleLength 4 -o output.csv

title: Fix Kanban cycle_length string type error in Python
description: When running Python sprint report in Kanban mode, cycle_length is being passed as a string to timedelta(weeks=...), causing 'unsupported type for timedelta weeks component: str' error. Groovy version works fine. Need to ensure cycle_length is converted to int.

title: Clean up Kanban board error handling in Python
description: Match Groovy behavior by catching RuntimeError in __main__ block instead of showing full traceback. When run against Kanban board, should show clean one-line message instead of stack trace.

title: Add OAuth refresh token support
description:
Implement silent token refresh using GitHub refresh tokens.

Currently, when an access token expires, the code initiates a new Device Flow (requires user interaction). GitHub OAuth Apps with token expiration enabled return both access_token AND refresh_token — the refresh token can silently obtain a new access token without user interaction.

Requirements:
- Store refresh_token in the cache alongside access_token
- When access_token is expired/expiring, use refresh_token to get new token silently
- Only fall back to Device Flow if refresh_token is also missing/expired
- Fix incorrect 8-hour default expiration assumption (tokens without expires_in should not expire)
- Update cache file schema: {access_token, refresh_token, expires_at, cached_at}

Implementation notes:
- Token refresh endpoint: https://github.com/login/oauth/access_token
- Use grant_type: refresh_token
- Refresh tokens expire after 6 months but renew on each use
- Access tokens expire every 8 hours (if enabled on the app)

Test with both token expiration enabled and disabled scenarios.

title: Apply GitHub OAuth pattern to Groovy implementation
description:
Port the GitHub OAuth implementation from Python to Groovy.

Implement the same Device Flow OAuth authentication pattern in the Groovy version:
- Create GitHubOAuth.groovy with Device Flow support
- Implement token caching to ~/.managertools/github_token.json
- Add automatic token refresh when approaching expiration
- Update RestService.groovy to handle SSO errors gracefully
- Fall back to manual token entry if OAuth fails
- Test with actual GitHub SSO requirements

This uses the same approach as the Python implementation:
- Client ID: Ov23liZxN05OIBtFKNTL
- Device Flow endpoints for non-interactive auth
- Local JSON-based token cache with expiration tracking
close_reason: Groovy is deprecated; not worth backporting OAuth

title: Implement GitHub OAuth with token caching and refresh
description:
Add GitHub OAuth support to handle SSO authentication requirements. Implement token caching and automatic refresh to avoid user interaction.

Requirements:
- Use Device Flow for OAuth (no browser interaction needed)
- Cache tokens locally (~/.managertools/github_token.json)
- Automatic token refresh when approaching expiration
- Fall back to manual token if OAuth fails
- Support both Bitbucket and GitHub authentication

Implementation approach:
1. Implement in Python first
2. Apply same pattern to Groovy
3. Integrate into authentication startup process

Current situation:
- Both Python and Groovy versions detect SSO requirements but don't implement OAuth
- Users encounter 403 SSO errors when GitHub requires authorization
- Groovy RestService.groovy has SSO detection but no OAuth handling
- Python rest_service.py has similar SSO detection

title: Fix data handling in SprintReportTeamAnalysis
description: Issue processing in SprintReportTeamAnalysis is failing with 'list' object has no attribute 'get' errors. This appears to be a data format issue where some Jira/GitHub API responses come back as lists instead of dicts. Issues are being silently dropped during processing, resulting in empty CSV output. Needs investigation into API response handling.

title: Port IndividualContributionsAnalysis to Python
description:
Port IndividualContributionsAnalysis.groovy (~1,156 lines) to Python. Second major analysis class after SprintReportTeamAnalysis.

Key components:
- Similar structure to SprintReportTeamAnalysis
- aggregateData() with issue/PR/commit aggregation
- Parallel processing with ThreadPoolExecutor
- Dense JSON navigation and counter increments
- CSV output generation

Depends on completion of SprintReportTeamAnalysis patterns.
acceptance_criteria: Analysis class ported with tests passing

title: Port SprintReportTeamAnalysis to Python
description:
Port SprintReportTeamAnalysis.groovy (~1,050 lines) to Python. This is the first of two main analysis classes.

Key components:
- Constants for user filtering, merge commit detection, PR tracking
- aggregateData() with SCRUM/KANBAN support and caching
- getIssueCategoryInformation() with nested PR/commit/comment processing
- generateOutput() producing per-sprint and overall CSV totals
- Helper methods: processDiffs(), processComment() (recursive), incrementCounter()
- CommentBlocker inner class for tracking
- Parallel issue processing with ThreadPoolExecutor

This is high-volume code with extensive JSON dot-notation navigation (~1500 lines similar patterns in the second analysis file).
acceptance_criteria: Analysis class ported with tests passing

title: Port ManagerTools to Python
description: Port the entire Groovy codebase (~4600 lines, 25 files) to Python. All output goes under python/ at repo root to avoid conflicts with existing Groovy source. Order: FlexiDB -> RestService/Auth -> REST clients -> AbstractSprintReport -> analysis files.
notes:
Phase 4 complete: CLI infrastructure (ConfigFileManager, CommandLineHelper, NullREST, GetTeamSprints, AbstractSprintReport) with 16 new tests. All 55 tests passing.

Completed phases:
1. FlexiDB (indexed in-memory database)
2. RestService/AuthInfo (HTTP client and auth)
3. REST clients (GitHub, Bitbucket, Jira)
4. CLI infrastructure (abstract base + config/prompts)

Remaining:
5. Analysis files (SprintReportTeamAnalysis ~1050 lines, IndividualContributionsAnalysis ~1150 lines) - high volume of dot-notation JSON navigation

title: Apply suggestions from review
Review entire code base for suggested refactors
notes: Full audit complete. 50+ issues found across 10 categories. Top priorities: (1) Massive code duplication in IndividualContributionsAnalysis - processGitHubRepository/processBitBucketRepository are 140+ lines of near-identical code; (2) getIssueCategoryInformation() in SprintReportTeamAnalysis is 250 lines - needs split; (3) Pattern.quote() security fix in JiraCopyTicketEstimates; (4) Delete unused getRequestConfig() in RestService; (5) Apply @Canonical to FlexiDBQueryColumn; (6) Use Groovy sort idiom in JiraREST; (7) Remove commented-out dead code; (8) Unsafe null checks missing in several places. Full issue list recorded in ManagerTools-0yu via apply-suggestions task.

title: Revisit REST read timeout (currently 60s)
description: The read timeout in RestService._execute_request() is set to (2, 60) — 2s connect, 60s read. This was likely set conservatively. Both REST and GraphQL calls share this timeout. 60s is probably too long; a hung request will block for a full minute before retrying. Should evaluate a more appropriate value (e.g. 15-30s) and consider whether GraphQL calls warrant a different timeout than REST calls given they batch more work per request.

title: Implement parallel commit diff fetching to reduce wall time for large PRs
description:
## Problem
PRs with many commits (e.g. 250) make N sequential get_commit_diffs() HTTP calls, causing multi-minute wall time that looks like a hang. With 60s read timeout per call, worst case is hours.

## Why not skip per-commit diffs above a threshold?
Option C (skip diffs above N commits, fall back to PR-level diff) was evaluated but ruled out because it conflicts with:
- ignoreCommitMessageContent commit-level filtering (downmerge skipping)
- maxCommitSize per-commit size check
- Future Kanban cycle attribution (ManagerTools-6rc) which needs per-commit timestamps + diffs

## Solution: Parallel commit diff fetching
Use a thread pool inside the commit loop to fetch get_commit_diffs() in parallel, then process results.

Key design constraints:
- The diff FETCH (HTTP call) can be parallelized
- The database WRITE (self.database.increment_field, etc.) needs to remain thread-safe (FlexiDB already has a global lock, but process_diffs calls increment_counter which calls database ops - need to verify)
- The existing -mt N threading is issue-level; this is commit-level within a single PR
- GitHub connection pool is 10 (rest_service.py pool_maxsize=10), so max useful parallelism is ~10 threads

Approach: collect (commit, index_lookup, diffs_response) tuples in parallel, write to DB sequentially after all fetches complete. Or verify FlexiDB lock granularity is sufficient for concurrent writes.

## Expected impact
- 250-commit PR: ~250 sequential calls → ~25 serial rounds with 10 threads = ~10x wall time reduction
- Does NOT reduce total API call count (no rate limit benefit)
- All per-commit filtering (message patterns, maxCommitSize) preserved

## Related beads
- ManagerTools-l9q: background performance investigation
- ManagerTools-6rc: Kanban attribution redesign (needs per-commit data, conflict with Option C)
close_reason: Superseded by GraphQL implementation (commit 3563f52) which eliminates per-commit REST calls entirely, providing better performance than parallelism

title: Kanban: attribute commits to the cycle they occurred in (not resolution cycle)
description:
## Problem
Current Kanban mode attributes ALL of a ticket's commits to the cycle when the ticket is *resolved*, regardless of when the commits were made. This means a ticket open for 6 months shows all its work in the final cycle, making earlier cycles look empty.

## Desired Behavior
Commits should be attributed to the cycle whose date window contains the commit timestamp — same behavior as sprint mode already uses.

## Design
Full plan at: /Users/human/.claude/plans/we-re-working-on-the-serialized-cerf.md

Key changes:
1. New Jira method get_kanban_window() — one JQL call for the full analysis window (not N resolution-date calls)
2. Precompute all cycle boundaries as [{name, start_ms, end_ms}]
3. Process each ticket once; each commit gets _find_cycle_for_timestamp() lookup → attributed to that cycle's row
4. Remove the mode != Mode.KANBAN timestamp-skip in process_pull_request (lines ~802, ~819)
5. Cache: one entry for full window instead of N per-cycle entries
6. Bump CACHE_VERSION to 1.4

## Note
This is a correctness fix, not a performance fix. GitHub API call volume is unchanged. Sprint (Scrum) mode is unaffected.

title: Investigate performance for large-commit PRs (250+ commits)
description:
## Problem
Script appears to hang on PRs with many commits (~250). Root cause is 250+ sequential HTTP calls (one get_commit_diffs per commit) with no progress output and a 60s read timeout per call.

## Key Findings

**HTTP layer (dominant factor):**
- 250 commits = ~253 sequential HTTP calls per PR
- No per-commit progress output — appears to hang silently
- At 1-2s/call: 4-8 min per large PR; at timeout ceiling: hours

**Business logic (secondary):**
- FlexiDB _find_rows does an O(n) scan per lookup behind a global lock; as DB grows (50 issues × 5 PRs × 250 commits = 62K rows) each lookup gets slower
- process_diffs has a triple-nested loop (files → hunks → segments) — called 250× per large PR
- Existing -mt N multithreading is issue-level only; single 250-commit PR still blocks its thread

## Options

- **A - Per-commit parallelism:** Thread pool inside commit loop; ~10x speedup but needs thread-safe DB writes
- **B - Cap commits per PR:** Slice to latest N commits; trivial but drops old commit data
- **C - Skip per-commit diffs above threshold:** Fall back to PR-level diff for big PRs; ~50x speedup, loses per-committer line attribution on those PRs
- **D - Progress output:** Print every N commits; no speed gain but eliminates apparent hang UX

## Recommendation
D + C: add progress output and skip per-commit diffs above a configurable threshold (e.g. 50 commits).

title: Create Python port documentation and setup guide
description:
Create comprehensive documentation for running the Python ManagerTools port.

Scope:
- Update requirements.txt with all Python dependencies (requests/httpx, pytest, etc.)
- Create python/README.md with setup and usage instructions:
  - Virtual environment setup
  - Dependency installation
  - How to run each analysis class (once ported)
  - Configuration file location and format
  - Authentication setup (Jira, GitHub, Bitbucket)
- Add 'Python Port' section to CLAUDE.md explaining:
  - Architecture differences from Groovy
  - Module structure
  - Running tests
- Create example scripts showing:
  - FlexiDB usage patterns
  - How to instantiate and run analysis classes
  - Output format examples

Target: Completion after SprintReportTeamAnalysis and IndividualContributionsAnalysis are ported.
close_reason: Root README covers execution, auth, and setup sufficiently
